stage-intentionally.label: Stage One Purpose at a Time
stage-intentionally.summary: Separate two supplied community-project purposes into focused snapshots.

sketch0.title: One Project Can Hold Several Purposes

sketch1.title: Stage the First Purpose by Name

try_stage_intentionally_sketch0.title: Stage Only the Schedule Purpose
try_stage_intentionally_sketch0.prompt: `community-project/schedule.txt` contains the supplied cleanup-time change, and `contacts.txt` contains the supplied help-desk change. Stage only `schedule.txt`. Leave the contact change in the working tree.
try_stage_intentionally_sketch0.hint: Name the schedule file explicitly so the unrelated contact edit stays unstaged.

help.concept: Intentional staging separates unrelated work before a commit exists.
help.hint_2: contacts.txt should remain only in the working tree.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- community-project
mkdir -p -- community-project
git -C community-project init -q -b main
git -C community-project config --local user.name 'ZoeSkoul Learner'
git -C community-project config --local user.email learner@zoeskoul.local
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared schedules and contact notes.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community schedule

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community contacts

Help desk: 555-0100
' > community-project/contacts.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Old planning notes

Spring flyer draft.
' > community-project/old-notes.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project/generated
printf %s 'Generated summary

Created automatically from project notes.
' > community-project/generated/summary.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-01T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-01T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Start community project'
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community schedule

Saturday cleanup: 9:30 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community contacts

Help desk: 555-0130
' > community-project/contacts.txt

try_stage_intentionally_sketch0.instructions: `community-project/schedule.txt` contains the supplied cleanup-time change, and `contacts.txt` contains the supplied help-desk change. Stage only `schedule.txt`. Leave the contact change in the working tree.

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you stage only the schedule purpose and leave the contact edit unstaged?

try_stage_intentionally_sketch1.title: Review the Proposed Schedule Snapshot
try_stage_intentionally_sketch1.prompt: The schedule change is staged and the contact change is not. Inspect only the staged patch for `schedule.txt`.
try_stage_intentionally_sketch1.hint: Review the staging area, not the remaining working-tree edit.

help.concept: git diff --staged shows what the next commit would record.
help.hint_1: Only schedule.txt should appear in the staged patch.
help.hint_2: The contacts.txt change must stay outside the proposed snapshot.

try_stage_intentionally_sketch1.instructions: The schedule change is staged and the contact change is not. Inspect only the staged patch for `schedule.txt`.

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you inspect only the staged schedule patch?

try_stage_intentionally_sketch2.title: Commit the Schedule Separately
try_stage_intentionally_sketch2.prompt: Record the staged schedule change with the message `Update community schedule`. Then read short status and confirm that `contacts.txt` is still modified but unstaged.
try_stage_intentionally_sketch2.hint: A focused commit may intentionally leave unrelated work unfinished.

help.concept: A focused commit can leave unrelated working-tree changes for a later snapshot.
help.hint_2: After the commit, contacts.txt should still appear modified.

try_stage_intentionally_sketch2.solutionCode:
git commit -m "Update community schedule"
git status --short

try_stage_intentionally_sketch2.instructions: Record the staged schedule change with the message `Update community schedule`. Then read short status and confirm that `contacts.txt` is still modified but unstaged.

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you record the staged schedule purpose with the requested message?

1.message: Did you confirm that the contact edit still remains unstaged?

try_stage_intentionally_sketch3.title: Record the Contact Change Separately
try_stage_intentionally_sketch3.prompt: Stage `contacts.txt`, inspect its staged patch, and record it with the message `Update community contacts`. The two purposes should now appear as separate commits.
try_stage_intentionally_sketch3.hint: Prepare and record only the remaining contact update.

help.concept: The second focused commit completes the unrelated contact change without mixing it into the schedule commit.
help.hint_1: Review the staged patch before recording it.
help.hint_2: The final message is Update community contacts.

try_stage_intentionally_sketch3.solutionCode:
git add contacts.txt
git diff --staged -- contacts.txt
git commit -m "Update community contacts"

try_stage_intentionally_sketch3.instructions: Stage `contacts.txt`, inspect its staged patch, and record it with the message `Update community contacts`. The two purposes should now appear as separate commits.

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you stage the remaining contact file?

1.message: Did you inspect its staged patch?

2.message: Did you record it as a separate purpose?

single-choice-1.title: Choose a Focused Snapshot
single-choice-1.prompt: Two unrelated files are modified. What best keeps the next commit focused?
single-choice-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Build the next snapshot one purpose at a time, review the staged version, and reverse a staging decision without losing work.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: To prepare selected changes for a commit
options.d: To list the recorded commit history

multi-choice-1.title: A Coherent Snapshot
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which practices help keep a commit focused? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: Judge each option independently; choose only the statements that stay true in this exact repository state.

help.concept: Build the next snapshot one purpose at a time, review the staged version, and reverse a staging decision without losing work.
help.hint_1: A partly true option is still wrong if it adds an effect Git does not guarantee.
help.hint_2: Use the layer named in the prompt—working tree, staging area, or history—to test each choice.

options.a: Stage files that serve the same project result.
options.b: Review the staged snapshot before committing.
options.c: Stage every changed file so status becomes quiet.
options.d: Use a subject that describes the result.

multi-choice-2.title: Inspect the Staged Boundary
multi-choice-2.prompt: Which commands can help verify what is staged?
multi-choice-2.hint: Choose the one answer that describes this command or concept precisely.

help.concept: Build the next snapshot one purpose at a time, review the staged version, and reverse a staging decision without losing work.
help.hint_1: Remove choices that act on a different repository layer or promise behavior Git does not provide.
help.hint_2: Prefer the exact definition used in this chapter over a nearby Git idea.

fill-blank-1.title: Write a Focused Commit Subject
fill-blank-1.prompt: The staged change expands the community cleanup time. Complete the subject: `git commit -m "[blank1]"`.
fill-blank-1.hint: Choose the term or symbol that matches the exact state described, not a nearby Git concept.

help.concept: Build the next snapshot one purpose at a time, review the staged version, and reverse a staging decision without losing work.
help.hint_1: Pay attention to whether the prompt describes an untracked path, a working-tree edit, staged work, or history.
help.hint_2: Read spaces, punctuation, and status columns as meaningful when the question shows Git output.

stage-intentionally-sketch0.title: One Project Can Hold Several Purposes
stage-intentionally-sketch0.bodyMarkdown: The schedule time and help-desk number are unrelated changes. They may exist together in the working tree without belonging in the same commit.

stage-intentionally-sketch1.title: Stage the First Purpose by Name
stage-intentionally-sketch1.bodyMarkdown: Naming `schedule.txt` stages only the cleanup-time change. `contacts.txt` remains outside the proposed snapshot.

stage-intentionally-sketch2.title: Review the Staged Snapshot
stage-intentionally-sketch2.bodyMarkdown: `git diff --staged -- path` shows what the next commit would record. Ordinary `git diff` answers a different question about unstaged work.

stage-intentionally-sketch3.title: Commit, Then Continue
stage-intentionally-sketch3.bodyMarkdown: After the schedule commit, the contact edit remains available for its own snapshot. This produces history whose entries each explain one purpose.
